title: Why you shouldn't hold in natural urges
excerpt: Ayurveda calls the suppression of natural urges "vega-dharana," and the classical texts warn that holding in urine, stool, hunger, or even a sneeze disturbs vata and sets off a chain of health troubles. Here is what that means for your daily life.
body_html:
a habit most of us don't think twice about

You are in the middle of a meeting and you feel the urge to use the washroom. You ignore it. You are at the dinner table and a sneeze builds up, so you stifle it. You skip breakfast because the morning is busy. These small suppressions feel harmless, but Ayurveda has been cautioning against exactly this for over two thousand years.

The classical term is vega-dharana, meaning the wilful holding back of natural urges. "Vega" refers to any bodily impulse that arises naturally, and "dharana" means suppression or retention. The Caraka Samhita lists thirteen natural urges that the body produces for good reason and warns that interfering with them disturbs the body's fundamental balance (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 7).

the blocked energy turns around and moves in the wrong direction, carrying toxins or causing pain in places that seem unrelated to the original urge

what the classical texts actually say

Ayurveda understands the body through three governing forces: vata, pitta, and kapha. Of these, vata, the force of movement and communication, is the one most directly responsible for carrying out every natural urge. Urine moves downward because of vata. Food travels through the gut because of vata. A sneeze, a cough, a yawn, even tears: all are expressions of vata doing its coordinating work.

When you suppress an urge, you are essentially blocking vata's natural direction of movement. The Caraka Samhita is specific: holding urine can lead to pain in the bladder and groin, difficulty urinating, and headache; suppressing the urge to defecate can cause bloating, cramping, and over time constipation; holding back hunger and thirst weakens the digestive fire and produces a feeling of heaviness and malaise (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 7). The Ashtanga Hridaya echoes this framework and organises these urges into two groups: those that are suppressible under some circumstances (like crying in public, or breaking wind in company) and those that should never be held, especially urine, stool, flatus, and hunger (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 4).

The deeper point is that vata, once disturbed by repeated suppression, does not simply settle back on its own. The texts describe a process in which the blocked energy turns around and moves in the wrong direction, carrying toxins or causing pain in places that seem unrelated to the original urge. This is why, in classical Ayurvedic reasoning, a chronic headache or persistent lower-back pain is sometimes traced back to long-standing habits of holding urine or suppressing the urge to defecate.

gentle everyday habits to respect the body's signals

The practical guidance from these texts is straightforward, even if modern life makes it harder to follow. The first habit is simply to respond when a natural urge arises, especially for urination, defecation, and hunger. The Ashtanga Hridaya advises not waiting until the urge becomes painful or overwhelming, but attending to it in a reasonable time (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 4).

For digestion specifically, eating at regular times each day, and not skipping meals when genuinely hungry, helps maintain what Ayurveda calls agni, the digestive fire. A weakened agni, caused partly by irregular eating and suppressed hunger, is considered a root cause of many chronic complaints in the Caraka Samhita (Caraka Samhita, Chikitsasthana, Ch. 15).

It is equally useful to observe what you suppress out of habit rather than necessity. Some people habitually suppress sneezing in quiet rooms, hold their breath in stressful moments, or avoid drinking water when thirsty because they do not want to interrupt work. Noticing these patterns is the first step. Small structural changes, like keeping water within reach, scheduling a proper lunch break, or choosing a route to work that allows a washroom stop, often make it easy to honour the body's timing without disrupting the day.

when to see a doctor

Ayurvedic lifestyle guidance is helpful for day-to-day wellbeing, but some symptoms need medical attention. See a doctor or qualified vaidya promptly if you have persistent difficulty urinating or a complete inability to pass urine, blood in your urine or stool, severe abdominal or pelvic pain, or any symptom that is new, worsening, or worrying you.

Chronic constipation lasting more than a few weeks, repeated urinary tract infections, or unexplained pain in the lower back or lower abdomen are also worth discussing with a healthcare provider. This article is general information about the Ayurvedic perspective on natural urges. It is not a diagnosis and is not a substitute for medical care.

Try This Today

  • Respond within a few minutes: When you feel the urge to urinate or defecate, aim to attend to it within a few minutes rather than waiting until it becomes urgent or painful. The Ashtanga Hridaya's guidance is simply not to habitually postpone.
  • Set a regular meal window: Eat your main meals at consistent times each day, and do not skip meals when you feel genuinely hungry. Even a 30-minute lunch break taken at the same time daily helps stabilise the digestive fire.
  • Keep water at your desk: Many people suppress thirst because water is not close at hand. A full bottle in view makes it easy to drink when the urge arises rather than deferring it until you remember.
  • Notice what you suppress out of habit: For one day, observe which natural urges you hold back and why. Sneezing in a quiet room, yawning in a meeting, stepping away from work to use the washroom. Awareness of the habit is usually enough to start changing it.

metafields.dek: Ayurveda calls the suppression of natural urges "vega-dharana," and the classical texts warn that holding in urine, stool, hunger, or even a sneeze disturbs vata and sets off a chain of health troubles. Here is what that means for your daily life.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Suppressing natural urges like urination or hunger disturbs vata, the body's movement force, according to classical Ayurvedic texts.
The Caraka Samhita and Ashtanga Hridaya both warn that blocked urges can cause pain, bloating, and systemic imbalance over time.
Responding to the body's signals promptly and eating at regular times are the simplest protective habits the tradition recommends.

glossary_terms.definition: The wilful suppression of natural bodily urges; "vega" is an urge or impulse, "dharana" is holding or retention.
The Ayurvedic force governing all movement, communication, and elimination in the body; most directly disturbed by suppressing natural urges.
The digestive fire responsible for transforming food into nourishment; weakened by irregular eating and suppressed hunger.

sources: Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 7 (vega-dharana: urges and consequences of suppression)
Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 4 (suppressible and non-suppressible urges)
Caraka Samhita, Chikitsasthana, Ch. 15 (role of agni in health and disease)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "vega-dharana" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, vega-dharana is defined as: The wilful suppression of natural bodily urges; "vega" is an urge or impulse, "dharana" is holding or retention.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to respond within a few minutes. When you feel the urge to urinate or defecate, aim to attend to it within a few minutes rather than waiting until it becomes urgent or painful. The Ashtanga Hridaya's guidance is simply not to habitually postpone.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to set a regular meal window. Eat your main meals at consistent times each day, and do not skip meals when you feel genuinely hungry. Even a 30-minute lunch break taken at the same time daily helps stabilise the digestive fire.
